region_of_origin: Singapore / Southeast Asia (Nestlé product leveraging Milo brand equity)
food_subgroup: Ready-to-eat breakfast cereal — chocolate malt-flavoured, whole grain wheat and corn base, fortified with vitamins and minerals

ontology.foodon_label: breakfast cereal food product (closest parent — brand-specific entry not available in FoodOn)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn covers generic breakfast cereals (FOODON_03315091). No brand-specific entry exists for Nestlé Milo Cereal. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Breakfast cereals — ready-to-eat (FoodEx2 closest parent; chocolate malt-flavoured variant not separately coded)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers ready-to-eat breakfast cereals. No brand-specific Milo Cereal code exists; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for branded breakfast cereal product. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nestlé Milo Cereal is 30g dry (approximately 1.5 cups), providing 114 kcal, 8.1g sugar, and 24.3g carbohydrate before any milk is added.

nutrient_source.title: Nestlé Milo Cereal — Nestlé Singapore product Nutrition Information Panel (NIP)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Nestlé Singapore product label NIP for Milo Cereal. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the verified product label. This is a USDA Branded Food-equivalent manufacturer declaration for a Singapore-market packaged product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of chocolate malt-flavoured whole grain breakfast cereal — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~67 (medium) estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for chocolate malt-flavoured whole grain breakfast cereals. No direct GI test result for Nestlé Milo Cereal Singapore formulation was identified in the database. The estimate is based on the closest analogous product category (chocolate malt whole grain cereal). GL per 30g serving = (67 × 24.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.3. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (250mg/100g); partially offset by potassium (280mg/100g) and calcium (200mg/100g) from fortification
hypertension.guidance: At 75mg sodium per 30g serving, Milo Cereal is lower in sodium than many competing cereals (e.g., corn flakes ~300mg/100g); however, the potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable and calcium fortification supports vascular health — patients with hypertension may consume a standard 30g serving but should monitor total daily sodium from all sources and avoid adding salted milk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar (27g/100g = 8.1g per 30g serving) combined with medium GI (67) and strong health-halo misconception from the Milo brand
type2_diabetes.guidance: Milo Cereal is not recommended for Type 2 Diabetes management: a single 30g serving delivers 8.1g free sugar (approximately 2 teaspoons) and a glycemic load of 16.3 before milk is added, and the Milo brand association creates a clinically significant misconception that must be addressed directly with patients using quantitative evidence — a realistic 60g bowl provides 16.2g sugar, representing 65% of the WHO daily free sugar limit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (2.5g/100g), zero trans fat, high sugar driving triglyceride and inflammatory risk, partially offset by whole grain fibre (5g/100g) and B-vitamin fortification
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (0.75g per 30g serving) is low and trans fat is absent, which is favourable for cardiovascular risk; however, the high sugar content (27g/100g) poses a risk for elevated triglycerides and insulin resistance in cardiovascular patients, and the 5g/100g dietary fibre from whole grain provides modest LDL-lowering benefit — patients with CVD should limit serving size to 30g and pair with unsweetened low-fat milk rather than full-fat or sweetened alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine release; cocoa flavanol contribution is not clinically meaningful at cereal serving sizes
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high added sugar content (27g/100g) may promote systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; patients should limit consumption and prefer lower-sugar whole grain alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nestlé Milo Cereal is a ready-to-eat breakfast cereal sold in Singapore and Southeast Asia, formulated using the Milo chocolate malt flavouring system applied to a whole grain wheat and corn base. It is consumed dry or with milk and requires no preparation. The cereal has a crunchy texture with a sweet chocolate-malt flavour characteristic of the Milo brand. Per 100g it provides 380 kcal, 6.7g protein, 5.0g fat (2.5g saturated), 81g carbohydrate, and 27g sugar — one of the highest sugar contents among mainstream breakfast cereals in Singapore. The product is heavily fortified with B vitamins (thiamin, riboflavin, niacin, B6, B12, folate), vitamin D, iron, calcium, and zinc. The glycemic index is approximately 67 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of 16.3 per standard 30g serving. Clinically, Milo Cereal presents a significant Type 2 Diabetes counselling challenge in Singapore: the Milo brand carries an intense health halo derived from decades of sports sponsorship, school canteen presence, and childhood nutrition messaging, leading many patients and parents to believe the cereal is a healthy choice. The clinical reality — 8.1g free sugar per 30g serving, equivalent to 2 teaspoons of sugar before milk is added — must be communicated with specific quantitative evidence to overcome this misconception. A realistic 60g bowl delivers 16.2g sugar, representing 65% of the WHO daily free sugar limit. The product is labelled vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free. It is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and is a common children's breakfast food.
common_user_queries: is Milo cereal good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Nestlé Singapore product label NIP (manufacturer declaration), which is the authoritative source for a branded packaged food. All per-100g values are verified from the product label. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (confidence 0.9) as no direct GI test for this specific Singapore formulation was identified.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported on the Nestlé Singapore Milo Cereal product NIP and not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific branded product as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not declared on the product label and not available in approved sources for this formulation. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per product label declaration. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per product label declaration. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per product label (no vitamin A fortification declared). vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per product label (no vitamin C fortification declared). All other nutrient values are taken verbatim from the verified Nestlé Singapore product NIP. GI value of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chocolate malt-flavoured whole grain breakfast cereals; no direct GI test result for this specific Singapore formulation was identified. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as (67 × 24.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 16.3. The health halo associated with the Milo brand is a clinically significant counselling challenge for T2DM management in Singapore.

Added FoodEx2 code A000E (closest parent) with gap note to satisfy Q5 ontological alignment.
Populated full per_100g using RESULT template field names (energy_kcal, protein_g, etc.) from input per_100g values.
Documented all -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) in notes with reason and source consulted.
Documented confirmed-zero fields (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg) in notes.
